etymology.description: 伍(伍) (wǔ, 'military ranks') [phonetic phonetic component] + 戈(戈) (gē, 'spear') [semantic component] -> 戲 originally borrowing meaning from near homophone 麾(麾) (huī, 'military banner') for 'command' -> play
etymology.interpretation: 戲 (xì, 'play') combines the phonetic component 伍(伍) (wǔ, 'military ranks') and semantic component 戈(戈) (gē, 'spear') to denote the original sense of commanding or signaling troops, similar to 麾(麾) (huī, 'military banner'). Over time, this military signaling led to a sense of orchestrating or orchestrated performance, hence 'play'.

examples.chinese: 孩子们正在操场上玩戏(孩子們正在操場上玩戲)。
examples.pinyin: Háizimen zhèngzài cāochǎng shàng wán xì.
examples.english: The children are playing on the playground.

examples.chinese: 我喜欢看京戏(我喜歡看京戲)，尤其是传统的剧目(尤其是傳統的劇目)。
examples.pinyin: Wǒ xǐhuan kàn jīngxì, yóuqí shì chuántǒng de jùmù.
examples.english: I enjoy watching Peking opera, especially traditional performances.

examples.chinese: 今晚剧院里有一场戏(今晚劇院裡有一場戲)，一定很精彩(一定很精彩)。
examples.pinyin: Jīnwǎn jùyuàn lǐ yǒu yī chǎng xì, yídìng hěn jīngcǎi.
examples.english: There is a play in the theater tonight; it's sure to be spectacular.
